sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=37, col=18).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 63, level 2.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Clicking p square at (1,9) had NO effect - state unchanged. The top panel clicks created new squares but didn't advance the level. The M squares exploded into S dots when moved away from the blob. I'm stuck in a failure state. Let me try clicking the scattered S dots on the board (not the top panel) - maybe they need to be clicked to restore M squares or convert them to p squares.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 43 actions; a strong score needs about 42 or fewer. You are 1.0x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 42 actions returned the board to a state already seen 42 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 42 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Great progress! Clicking the S dot at (37, 18) converted it to an M square! Now I have:
- M: 2 (was 1, now 2)
- p: 1
- Y: 2
- S: 7 (was 9, now 7 - 2 fewer because one S dot became an M square)
- Blob: 69
So clicking the scattered S dots converts them to M squares! This is the same mechanic as level 1. I need to click all the remaining S dots to convert them to M squares.
The remaining S dots are at:
- (1, 1) - top panel S square (4 pixels)
- (37, 41)
- (40, 37)
- (41, 16)
- (54, 49)
- (55, 14)
- (56, 47)
- (57, 16)
That's 8 remaining S dots. But wait, the top panel S square at (1,1) is 4 pixels (2x2), while the others are 1 pixel each. The top panel one might be different.
Actually, looking at the count: before clicking, there were 9 S dots. After clicking (37, 18), there are 7 S dots. So 9 - 7 = 2 S dots were removed (the clicked one and... wait, that doesn't make sense).
Actually, 9 - 7 = 2, but I only clicked one S dot. So maybe clicking the S dot at (37, 18) removed 2 S dots (the clicked one and another one)? Or maybe the count is wrong.
Let me re-examine. Before: S dots = 9. After clicking (37, 18): S dots = 7. So 2 S dots were removed. But I only clicked one. Maybe the click removed the clicked S dot and created an M square, but the count shows 7 S dots remaining, which means 9 - 2 = 7. So 2 S dots were removed.
Wait, maybe the S dot at (37, 18) was removed, and another S dot was also removed (maybe it merged with the M square?). Or maybe the count is just wrong.
Actually, looking at the M count: before M = 1, after M = 2. So 1 new M square was created. The S count went from 9 to 7, which is a decrease of 2. So 2 S dots were removed, but only 1 M square was created. This doesn't add up.
Let me just continue clicking the remaining S dots to convert them to M squares. The goal might be to convert all S dots to M squares, and then do something with the M squares.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider. The S dots on the board are 1-pixel dots, while the M squares are 2x2 squares. Clicking a 1-pixel S dot creates a 2x2 M square. So the S dots are "seeds" that grow into M squares when clicked.
The remaining S dots (excluding the top panel one) are:
- (37, 41)
- (40, 37)
- (41, 16)
- (54, 49)
- (55, 14)
- (56, 47)
- (57, 16)
That's 7 S dots. Plus the top panel one at (1, 1), which is 4 pixels (2x2). So there are 8 remaining S dots total.
Let me click all of them to convert them to M squares. But wait, the top panel S square might be special (it's part of the HUD). Let me skip it for now and click the scattered ones first.
Actually, I should be careful about the efficiency budget. I've used 43 actions already, and the target is about 42. So I need to be efficient. Let me batch the remaining S dot clicks.
The remaining scattered S dots are at:
- (37, 41)
- (40, 37)
- (41, 16)
- (54, 49)
- (55, 14)
- (56, 47)
- (57, 16)
That's 7 clicks. Plus the top panel one at (1, 1), which I already clicked earlier (but it didn't convert to M, it created a p square). So maybe the top panel S square is different.
Let me click all 7 remaining scattered S dots to convert them to M squares.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: World model: Clicking S dot at (37, 18) converted it to an M square! M count went from 1 to 2, S count from 9 to 7. The mechanic is: click scattered S dots to convert them to M squares. Need to click remaining 7 scattered S dots to restore all M squares, then figure out what to do with them.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Click all remaining scattered S dots to convert them to M squares
# Remaining S dots (excluding top panel at 1,1):
# (37, 41), (40, 37), (41, 16), (54, 49), (55, 14), (56, 47), (57, 16)
s_dot_positions = [(37, 41), (40, 37), (41, 16), (54, 49), (55, 14), (56, 47), (57, 16)]
actions = [{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': r, 'col': c} for r, c in s_dot_positions]
result = action(actions)

seg = current_frame.segmentation
m_squares = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'M']
p_squares = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'p']
y_squares = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'Y']
s_dots = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'S' and n['pixels'] <= 4]
blob = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'b']

print(f"M: {len(m_squares)}, p: {len(p_squares)}, Y: {len(y_squares)}, S: {len(s_dots)}")
if blob:
    print(f"Blob: {blob[0]['pixels']}")
for n in m_squares:
    print(f"  M: {n['boundary']}")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
print(f"Done: {last_action_result.get('done', False)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
